---
title: "Inputs"
---

Propagate uses two inputs to generate the outputs described on the
[Outputs](/docs/design/interface/outputs.qmd) page. See the [Design overview
page](/docs/design/index.qmd) for a description of the user types, Requester and
Owner, referred to below.

The inputs include:

- A structured metadata file for the data package, for example the
  `datapackage.json` from the [Data Package](https://datapackage.org)
  specification. Owners use this file to generate a web application through
  which a Requester can create a data request.

<!-- TODO: Add `data-dict.yaml` as well? -->

- A data request, in a `request.yaml` file, made through the web application or
  terminal interface by a Requester. The request file includes a SQL-like query
  of selected resources, fields, and filters, as well as any additional metadata
  about the request (e.g., requester name, email, project name, and rationale).
  The Requester creates this file for a specific research project that needs the
  data. The Owner uses this file to create the subset of data from the data
  package.

<!-- TODO: Agree on what word(s) to use about the data request/subset/instructions form and align across design docs -->
<!-- TODO: Do we want to add a config so users can customise the content of the web application, as we in Flower? -->

We've designed Propgate with the expectation of a single `request.yaml` file for each research project within a
data package. A *research project* is a specific activity or study conducted by the Requester
that needs access to data from the data package. If a Requester forgets certain
columns or rows from the subset or needs different data, they would update their
existing `request.yaml` file and resubmit it to the Owner. There should only
ever be *one* `request.yaml` file related to a given research project. We've designed
Propagate this way to avoid confusion about different versions of a request or
which request file to run for a research project.
